---
title: "The IRFs package"
---

## Installation

To install the package
```julia
using Pkg
Pkg.add("https://github.com/gragusa/IRFs.jl")
```

```{julia}
using IRFs
```


## VAR

A Vector Autoregression (VAR) is 
$$
Y_t = c + A_1 Y_{t-1} + A_2 Y_{t-2} + \dots + A_p Y_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t, \quad t=p+1,\ldots, T.
$$
where $Y_t$ is $m\times 1$ vector of time-series, $c$ is a $m\times 1$ vector of constant, and $A_j$, $j=1,\ldots,p$, are $m\times m$ matrices of parameters.

To showcase the `IRFs.jl` package, we will use the Kilian and Kim dataset.

```{julia}
using CSV
using DataFrames
using Dates
using Test
using Plots
function get_data()
  path_d = @__DIR__
  @show path_d
  @show joinpath(path_d, "kilian_kim_original_dataset.csv")
  data = DataFrame(CSV.File(joinpath(path_d, "kilian_kim_original_dataset.csv"), header=false))
  names = [:CFNAI, :CPI, :PCOM, :FF]
  rename!(data, names)
  DY    = data[!, :CFNAI][2:end] # CFNAI
  DP    = (log.(data[!, :CPI][2:end]) - log.(data[!, :CPI][1:end - 1])) * 100  # D(log(CPI))
  DPCOM = ((log.(data[!, :PCOM][2:end]) - log.(data[!, :CPI][2:end])) - (log.(data[!, :PCOM][1:end - 1]) - log.(data[!, :CPI][1:end - 1]))) * 100 # D(log(real PCOM)): CRB
  FF    = data[!, :FF][2:end]  # FedFound
 return [DY DP DPCOM FF], names
end

Y, names = get_data()
#plot(Y; layout = (2,2))
```



### Estimation 
To estimate a VAR with $p=12$:

```{julia}
using IRFs
v = VAR(Y, 12, names = names, index = Date(1970,1,1):Month(1):Date(2007, 12, 1))
IRFs.fit!(v)
```


We can summarize the VAR:

```{julia}
summary(v)
```

## Impulse Response function

The impulse response function of the VAR using a Triangular identification scheme with UnitEffect normalization:

```{julia}
R = TriangularRestriction(v, UnitEffect())
irf_1 = impulseresponse(v, R; horizon=24, ci_type=:delta)
```

`ci_type` specifies the confidence interval types:
- `:delta`: delta method
- `:bootstrap`: standard bootstrap
- `:wildbootstrap`: wild bootstrap
- `:blockbootstrap`: block-bootstrap

For the case `:blockbootstrap`, there is an option `block_width` (default=10), which specifies the block width

```{julia}
bbirf = impulseresponse(v, R; horizon=24, ci_type=:blockbootstrap, block_width=11)
```

We can finally plot the IRF
```{julia}
plot(bbirf)
```

## Local projection

The type `LocalProjection` specifies the local projection. At the moment only the triangular identification is available\dots

```{julia}
lp = IRFs.LocalProjection(Y, 12; names = names)
```

Not `lp` is of type `LocalProjection`. The command `lp_irfs` estimates the following set of regressions 

$$
Y_{j,t+h}=\alpha^{(h)}+\beta^{(h)}Y_{s,t}+\sum_{\stackrel{i\leqslant m}{i\neq s}}\gamma_{i}^{(h)}R_{ji}Y_{i,t}+\sum_{r\leqslant p}\sum_{i\leqslant m}\delta_{ih}Y_{i,t-r}+u_{j,t+h}, j\leqslant m, s\leqslant m, h\leqslant H.
$$
The term $R_{ji}$ denote the element $(i,j)$ of the matrix $R$ used to identify the projection, that is, the matrix mapping structural errors in to innovations
$$
Ru_t = \varepsilon_t.
$$
$R$ has the diagonal equal to 1 (unit effect normalization). 

```{julia}
lp_irf = IRFs.impulseresponse_fit(lp, 24) ## 24 horizon
```
`lp_irf.ols` collects the regression. 

```{julia}
ols = lp_irf.ols;
```

So, the IRF of shock `j` on variable `i` at lag `h` is 
```{julia}
h = 1
i = 1
j = 2
coef(ols[i,j][j])[h]
```

The impulse response functions are collected in `irf`
```{julia}
lp_irfs = lp_irf.irf
```

The impulse to variable `j` to a shock `s` at horizon `h` is

```{julia}
j = 2
s = 1
h = 1
lp_irfs[j, s, h]
```

Alterntevely, we can use the following convention
```{julia}
lp_irfs[variable=:CPI, shock=:FF, horizon=1:25]
```


By default, the `lpirf` calculates the SE using the White variance estimator (HR1()). 

```{julia}
se_lp_irf = lp_irf.se
```

The standard errors can be accessed using the same convention of the impulse responses.
```{julia}
se_lp_irf[variable=:CPI, shock=:FF, horizon=1:25]
```

We can plot the IRFs using 
```{julia}
plot(lp_irf)
```

To update the local projection irf's se using different estimator is possible using `confidencebands!`. 
```{julia}
k = Kernels([Bartlett(max(0,h-1)) for h in 0:24]);
IRFs.confidencebands!(lp_irf, k) ## HAC with Bartlett kernel with optimal bandwidth
```

Now we can plot using the se that uses the Bartlett kernel with a Bandwidth that is equal to `h`
```{julia}
plot(lp_irf)
```


### Monte Carlo simulations

To simulate a VAR using an estimated one, we can use
`simulate!`. 

Let `YY` be the matric the will host the simulated variables

```{julia}
p = 12 ## Important
YY = Matrix{Float64}(undef, 1500 + p, 4)
fill!(YY, 0.0)
YYY = copy(YY)
```

We can simulate using the coefficient and the variance of the innovation
```{julia}
using LinearAlgebra
C = cholesky(v.Sigma_e)
epsilon = randn(1500, 4)*C.U;

## :leavealone leaves the first p=12 alone
## since they are zero, we are setting them equal
## the unconditional mean (the VAR is estimated in 
## deviation from the mean)
```

```{julia}
simulate!(YY, epsilon, v, :mean)
IRFs.simulates!(YYY, epsilon, v, :mean)
```

```{julia}
v_ = VAR(YY, 12, names = [:CFNAI, :CPI, :PCOM, :FF])
```
